desc: Goa is India's best-known beach destination, with clear seasonality: sunny, high-demand winter months versus a rain-heavy monsoon that brings quieter beaches and lower rates. It works for both short high-season breaks and slower shoulder/monsoon stays depending on priorities.
hls: Good shoulder season value in March and October

todo.name: North Goa beach circuit
todo.desc: Move between Vagator, Morjim, and Ashwem based on swell and vibe.

todo.name: Fontainhas heritage walk
todo.desc: Explore Panaji's Latin Quarter, old houses, and galleries.

todo.name: Spice farm or hinterland village trip
todo.desc: Balance beach days with inland greenery and Goan cuisine.

todo.name: Sunset cruise or riverfront evening
todo.desc: Catch Mandovi or Chapora estuary light and cooler evening temps.

todo.name: South Goa slow day
todo.desc: Swap to quieter beaches like Palolem or Agonda for a calmer pace.

todo.name: Goan food trail
todo.desc: Try fish thali, cafreal, xacuti, and local bakeries across districts.

wifi.note: Good in major beach hubs and newer stays; outages still occur in smaller villages during heavy monsoon weather.

prac.fltNote: Gatwick routings usually connect via major Middle East or India hubs into GOX or GOI. Return fares are often ~£380–£620 off-peak, ~£620–£900 in shoulder demand, and ~£900+ around Christmas/New Year and winter peak weeks.
prac.lang: Konkani, Marathi, Hindi, English widely used
prac.visa: India eVisa is available for many nationalities; confirm current entry category and validity before travel.
alerts: Monsoon seas can be rough with swim restrictions on some beaches.
Christmas/New Year periods have strong price spikes and transport demand.
Road traffic can be heavy around airport corridors and popular beach belts in peak season.

sweet: November to February is the classic beach season; March and October can offer better value with still-usable weather.
